summary.category: Money as Solution to Barter and Modern Forms
points: Barter's 'double coincidence of wants' problem—requiring simultaneous mutual desire—made it impractical. Money solved this by acting as universal medium of exchange. Modern money exists in two forms: currency (notes/coins issued by RBI) and demand deposits (bank deposits withdrawable on demand via cheques). Banks are central to modern money system, accepting deposits, lending them out, and enabling transactions through cheques—making them essential intermediaries in the economy.

summary.category: Credit System: Formal vs Informal and Inequality
points: Credit is essential for economic activity but creates inequality. Formal sector (banks, cooperatives) provides regulated credit at reasonable rates (8-12% annually) with protections. Informal sector (moneylenders, traders) charges 2-5 times higher rates (10-30% monthly), creating debt traps. Access is unequal: rich households access 83% from formal sources; poor access only 17% formally, forced into expensive informal credit. High informal rates trap borrowers in perpetual debt, preventing productive investment.

Self-Help Groups (SHGs) and Grameen Bank models demonstrate that poor borrowers are reliable if credit terms are appropriate. SHGs use group savings and collective credibility to substitute for individual collateral, enabling poor women to access credit at reasonable rates. Expansion of formal credit with simplified procedures, direct lending to poor without collateral requirements, and support for SHGs is essential for inclusive development—enabling poor to invest productively and escape poverty traps created by informal credit.
